My Team Had a Production Incident Every Three Weeks for a Year. Then One Engineer Asked a Different Question.

The incidents were not the problem. The question we were not asking at the beginning of each one was.

9 min read
Just now

--

For twelve months, we averaged one significant production incident every three weeks.
I tracked them on a spreadsheet. Not because I was required to. Because I needed to understand if there was a pattern I was missing, or if we were just unlucky, or if the system had a fundamental problem that the postmortems were failing to surface.
Thirty-seven entries over twelve months. Different services. Different root causes. Different engineers on the bridge each time. Different postmortem action items, most of which were completed, some of which probably prevented specific recurrences.
And still, every three weeks, something broke.
I had stopped believing it was bad luck by month four. I had stopped believing it was the specific root causes by month seven, when we had fixed the root causes from the previous six months and new ones kept appearing. By month ten I had started to believe it was something structural about how we were responding to incidents, not something structural about the systems themselves.
I was right. I just didn’t know what the structural thing was until a junior engineer named Yael asked a question in month eleven that I had never heard anyone ask on a production bridge before.

What the First Ten Minutes of Every Incident Looked Like
I sat in enough of our incident bridges to recognize the pattern by month six.
The alert fired. Engineers joined the bridge. Someone opened the failing service’s dashboard. Someone else pulled the logs. A third person checked the recent deployment history. A fourth reached out to the database team because the errors mentioned the database.
Everyone was doing something. The bridge was active. The investigation was underway.
Nobody had established what kind of problem we were looking for.
That distinction — between doing something and knowing what kind of something to do — is the distinction I could not articulate until Yael made it visible by doing the opposite.
The searches were not coordinated. They were parallel individual efforts, each engineer following their own instincts about where the cause was most likely to be. Sometimes those instincts were right. Often they were not, and twenty minutes would pass before the collective investigation realized it had been searching in the wrong category.
Twenty minutes multiplied by thirty-seven incidents is twelve hours of combined engineering time spent searching in the wrong place. That is the conservative estimate. The actual number was higher because sometimes the wrong category held the investigation for forty minutes, sometimes for an hour.
The incidents did not last as long as they did because the causes were hard to find. They lasted as long as they did because we started looking before we had defined what we were looking for.

The Engineer Who Asked the Different Question
Yael had been on my team for seven months when the incident in month eleven happened.
She was not the most senior engineer on the bridge. She was not the incident commander. She did not have the deepest knowledge of the failing service.
She joined the bridge four minutes after the alert fired, which meant four minutes of investigation had already happened in her absence. Engineers were already in the logs. The database team had already been paged. A hypothesis about a recent deployment was already being tested.
She did not open a dashboard. She did not pull logs. She asked a question.
“Before we keep going — is this affecting all users or a specific subset?”
The bridge went quiet for a moment that felt longer than it was.
Nobody knew. We had been investigating for four minutes and nobody had established the scope of the failure.
Someone checked. The failure was affecting a specific subset. Users who had updated their account settings in the last six hours. Everyone else was unaffected.
That answer, which took forty-five seconds to establish, eliminated every hypothesis currently being investigated on the bridge. The deployment that was being tested had happened eighteen hours ago. The database issue that the database team was looking at affected all users equally. Both investigations were in the wrong place.
Yael asked two more questions. When had the account settings update feature last been modified. And what did the error message say specifically, not generically.
The answers took three minutes to surface. They pointed directly at a migration that had run six hours earlier, affecting only the accounts that had been updated since the migration. The migration had modified a column that the account settings feature was reading with an assumption that was no longer valid.
Total time from alert to root cause: eleven minutes.
Average time for our previous thirty-six incidents: sixty-three minutes.

What She Was Doing That Nobody Else Was
I debriefed Yael the next day. Not to give feedback. To understand.
She described what she had done in terms that were almost embarrassingly simple.
Before looking at anything, she had asked herself what she needed to know to decide where to look. The answer to that question was always the same three things. Who was affected. When it started relative to what changed. What the error actually said versus what the alert headline said.
She had learned this, she told me, from reading postmortems from other companies. Not our postmortems. External ones, from organizations that published detailed incident analyses. She had noticed that the investigations that resolved fastest always established those three things in the first two minutes. The ones that took longest established them eventually, after following wrong hypotheses for a while, and the point at which the investigation turned was always the point at which those three things became clear.
So she had started doing it deliberately. Asking the three questions before opening anything. Resisting the pressure to look immediately, to be visibly doing something, to follow the natural instinct of the bridge which was toward action rather than toward orientation.
The discipline of the two-minute pause before investigation was the entire difference.
Not technical knowledge. Not system expertise. Not experience with this specific service or this specific failure mode.
Two minutes. Three questions. A defined search space before the search began.

Why We Had Kept Doing It Wrong
After that incident I went back through the thirty-six previous postmortems and looked for the moment in each one when the investigation changed direction.
It was there in thirty-two of them. A question that someone asked, or a piece of information that surfaced, that reoriented the investigation toward the right place. In most cases it appeared between twenty and forty minutes into the incident. In a few cases it appeared later.
In every case, the information that produced the reorientation was information that had been available from the beginning. The scope of the failure was determinable in the first two minutes. The timeline was reconstructable from the alert data and the deployment logs that everyone had access to. The specific error message was in the logs that had been open since minute one.
We had the information. We were not asking the questions that would have surfaced it first.
The reason was one I recognized as soon as I articulated it. Production incident bridges have a social pressure that is specific to them and powerful. The pressure is toward visible action. Toward doing something, immediately, because the system is down and every second costs something and the right response to urgency is to move fast.
That pressure is not wrong. Speed matters in incidents. But the pressure resolves toward action in the wrong direction when the direction of action has not been established. Moving fast in the wrong direction is not speed. It is wasted motion that looks like speed from the outside.
The two-minute pause felt, on the bridge, like slowing down. Like not responding to the urgency. Like being the person who asked questions when they should be looking at dashboards.
It was the fastest thing available. It just did not feel that way.

What Changed After Month Eleven
I made Yael the incident commander for the next incident that occurred after that conversation.
Not because she was the most senior engineer. Because she had demonstrated a framework that I wanted the team to see applied explicitly, by the person who had developed it, in a real incident.
The next incident happened eleven days later. Different service, different failure mode, different engineers on the bridge.
Yael opened the bridge with three questions. Scope, timeline, signal. Who is affected, when did it start relative to what changed, what does the specific error say.
The answers took ninety seconds. They eliminated two of the three hypotheses that had begun forming instinctively in the room. The remaining hypothesis was tested. It was correct. The incident resolved in fourteen minutes.
The engineer who had instinctively been moving toward one of the eliminated hypotheses told me afterward that watching the scope question eliminate his hypothesis in forty-five seconds was the most useful thing he had seen in a production incident in his career. Not because he had been wrong about his hypothesis, though he had been. Because he understood for the first time that the reason to ask the scope question was not to verify his hypothesis. It was to eliminate the hypotheses that were not worth pursuing.
That distinction — between confirming and eliminating — is the one that changed how the team investigated incidents after month eleven.

The Number That Changed
Our incident frequency in the twelve months after month eleven: nine.
Down from thirty-seven.
I want to be careful about the causal claim here. We made other changes. The systems matured. The team grew. There are multiple variables.
But the change in investigation behavior was the most significant structural change we made, and the incident frequency dropped most sharply in the two months immediately following that change, before the other variables had time to take effect.
Nine incidents in twelve months instead of thirty-seven. The same systems, mostly. The same engineers, mostly. A different first two minutes.
I think about that ratio when I hear engineering leaders talk about incident response as primarily a technical problem. As something that gets better as systems mature and reliability engineering practices improve and infrastructure stabilizes.
Those things matter. They are not the primary variable.
The primary variable in our incident frequency was not what we were building. It was how we were starting the investigation when something broke. We had been starting by looking. We should have been starting by asking. The difference between those two starting points, multiplied by thirty-seven incidents, was twenty-eight incidents that resolved in sixty-three minutes instead of eleven.

What I Tell New Engineers Before Their First On-Call Rotation
I have a conversation now with every engineer who is about to take their first on-call rotation.
It is not about the runbooks, though I walk them through those. It is not about the escalation path, though I explain that too. It is not about the specific failure modes they are likely to encounter, though I describe those.
It is about the first two minutes.
Before you open a dashboard. Before you pull logs. Before you page the database team. Ask three things.
Who is affected. Is this every user or a subset? Is this one region or all regions? Is this one endpoint or all traffic? The answer eliminates categories before you spend time in them.
When did it start relative to what changed. Not when the alert fired. When t
